distribution_analysis.most_covered_section: The Exponential Functions and Their Graphs

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: The Exponential Functions and Their Graphs
questions.question: What is the range of the function \(f(x) = 2^x\)?
questions.hint: 2^x is always positive for any real x.
questions.explanation: Exponential functions with positive base have range \((0, \infty)\). Option A is domain, Option D excludes values between 0 and 1 incorrectly.

source_trace.section_anchor: The Exponential Functions and Their Graphs
source_trace.content_anchor: range is set of positive real numbers

explanations_tiered.definition: Range of \(a^x\) with a>0, a≠1 is \((0,\infty)\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Exponential function never reaches zero and is always positive.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You can't get zero or negative from a positive number raised to any power.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since \(2^x > 0\) for all x and can be arbitrarily close to 0 or large, range is (0,∞).

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use graph to show values always positive.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify domain and range of exponential functions

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: The Logarithmic functions and their Graphs
questions.question: What is the vertical asymptote of \(y = \ln(x - 2)\)?
questions.explanation: The graph of \(y = \ln(x - 2)\) is shifted right 2 units, so vertical asymptote moves from x=0 to x=2. Option A is asymptote of ln x.

source_trace.section_anchor: The Logarithmic functions and their Graphs

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Horizontal shifts move the vertical asymptote.
explanations_tiered.beginner: For \(\ln(x-2)\), inside becomes 0 when x=2, so vertical line x=2 is asymptote.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\ln(x-2)\) undefined for x≤2, as x→2⁺, ln→ -∞, so x=2 vertical asymptote.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Parent function \(\ln x\) has asymptote x=0; shift right 2 → asymptote x=2.
reasoning_steps: That x-value is the vertical asymptote for log.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine asymptotes of shifted logarithmic graphs

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Simplify \((x^3)^4\).
questions.hint: Power of a power: multiply exponents.
questions.explanation: \((a^m)^n = a^{mn}\), so \((x^3)^4 = x^{3×4} = x^{12}\). Option A adds exponents (product rule), Option C multiplies incorrectly.

explanations_tiered.definition: Power of a power: multiply exponents.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When raising a power to another power, exponents multiply.
explanations_tiered.beginner: \((x^3)^4\) means multiply x^3 by itself 4 times: exponents add to 12.
explanations_tiered.advanced: General rule: \((a^m)^n = a^{mn}\) for integer exponents.
worked_solution: Step 1: Apply power rule: multiply exponents: 3 × 4 = 12.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Power of a power: multiply exponents.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Multiply exponents when raising a power to a power.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply power of a power rule

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Rewrite \(\log_5 25 - \log_5 5\) as a single logarithm.
questions.explanation: \(\log_5 25 - \log_5 5 = \log_5(25/5) = \log_5 5 = 1\). Option B is \(\log_5 5 = 1\). Option A would be product (add) or wrong operation.

explanations_tiered.definition: Quotient property: difference of logs = log of quotient.
reasoning_steps: Combine into single log with division.

glossary_entry.definition: Log of quotient = difference of logs.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: Relation Between Exponential and Logarithmic Functions with the same base
questions.subsection: Relation Between Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
questions.structural_anchor: Relation between exponential and logarithmic functions
questions.question: If \(f(x) = 5^x\) and \(g(x) = \log_5 x\), then what is \(g(f(2))\)?
questions.explanation: \(f(2) = 5^2 = 25\). Then \(g(25) = \log_5 25 = 2\). Option A is correct. Option D is \(f(2)\) not composed.

source_trace.section_anchor: Relation Between Exponential and Logarithmic Functions with the same base
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Relation Between Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
source_trace.structural_element: Relation between exponential and logarithmic functions

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Exponential and log with same base are inverses.

glossary_entry.definition: \(g(f(x)) = x\) when f and g are inverses.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Applying inverse functions returns original input.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that they cancel each other.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: The Exponential Functions and Their Graphs
questions.question: Evaluate \(e^{\ln 7}\).
questions.explanation: \(e^{\ln 7} = 7\) because exponential and natural log are inverses. Option B is \(e^1\), Option C is just ln 7.

source_trace.section_anchor: The Exponential Functions and Their Graphs

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Exponential base e and natural log undo each other.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The e and ln cancel, leaving 7.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Composition of inverse functions yields identity on domain.
reasoning_steps: Apply inverse property: e^{ln a} = a.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Stress that ln undoes e and vice versa.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: The Logarithmic functions and their Graphs
questions.question: Simplify \(\ln(e^4)\).
questions.explanation: \(\ln(e^4) = 4\) because natural log and e are inverses. Option B is argument, Option D would be \(\ln e = 1\).

source_trace.section_anchor: The Logarithmic functions and their Graphs

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Logarithm base e of e raised to power returns exponent.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use the fact that ln and e are inverses.

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Express RHS as power of 2, then solve linear equation.

glossary_entry.definition: Equation where both sides can be written with same base.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What principal must be invested at 5% annual interest compounded annually to reach 1000 birr after 2 years?

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Solve for principal given future amount.
reasoning_steps: Set up equation with given values.
misconceptions: Using A = P(1+rt) for simple interest.

glossary_entry.definition: Principal required today to grow to A at interest rate r over t years.

flashcard.front: Find P if A=500, r=0.1, t=2, compounded annually.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find P if A=2000, r=0.05, t=3.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Principal that grows to A at rate r over t years.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: The Logarithmic functions and their Graphs
questions.question: Which of the following is the inverse of \(y = 10^x\)?
questions.hint: The inverse of an exponential function is a logarithm with the same base.
questions.explanation: Inverse of \(y = a^x\) is \(y = \log_a x\). So inverse of \(y = 10^x\) is \(y = \log_{10} x\). Option D would be base change.

source_trace.section_anchor: The Logarithmic functions and their Graphs

explanations_tiered.definition: Inverse of exponential \(a^x\) is logarithmic \(\log_a x\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For base 10, the inverse is common logarithm.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Exponential and log with same base undo each other.

publishing_metadata.revision_note: Inverse of a^x is log_a x.
